what: Why the paper (and the package default) prefers IPWRA
title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: AIPW achieves double robustness adding correction term, sum can wander outside logical range outcome — predicted probability 1.3, negative count. IPWRA achieves reweighting quasi-likelihood regression, long choose sensible family (logit binary outcomes, Poisson counts), fitted values stay range construction. Hence IPWRA’s appealing small-sample behavior paper’s simulations, place package default. second practical lesson paper concerns normalization. Raw IPW weights Zi/p̂(Xi)Z_i/\hat p(X_i) need average one sample; observations small estimated propensity scores, unnormalized estimators can erratic. Normalizing weights sum one (Hájek construction) fixes costs nothing, normalized = TRUE default. matters "ipw" "aipw"; IPWRA normalized construction.

title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: Three formulas, one per model: outcome model, treatment model, instrument propensity score model. Read three rows bottom-: ATE: Z D — first stage: instrument moves treatment probability 0.60 (complier share). ATE: Z Y — intent--treat effect instrument outcome. LATE: D Y — ratio, causal effect compliers. 95% interval comfortably covers true 0.5. final line reports first-stage strength: single binary instrument, squared z-statistic first-stage robust F. F ≈ 1084, far conventional F = 10 danger threshold, Wald inference ratio trustworthy. Section 7 shows package isn’t. standard errors afterthought: drlate stacks every estimation stage (propensity score, outcome regressions, treatment regressions, ratio ) one moment system computes joint sandwich variance. uncertainty estimating propensity score propagates LATE’s standard error, exactly Stata original.
code: fit <- drlate(outcome    = lwage   ~ age + educ,               treatment  = nvstat  ~ age + educ,               instrument = rsncode ~ age + educ,               data = drlate_sim) summary(fit) #>  #> Local average treatment effect #> Number of obs    : 2,000 #> Estimator        : IPWRA #> Outcome model    : linear #> Treatment model  : logit #> Instrument model : logit (MLE) #>  #>              Estimate Std. Error z value   Pr(>|z|) [95% conf. interval] #> LATE: D on Y   0.4705    0.07915   5.944  2.786e-09     0.3153    0.6256 #> ATE: Z on Y    0.2845    0.05043   5.642  1.679e-08     0.1857    0.3834 #> ATE: Z on D    0.6048    0.01837  32.929 8.326e-238     0.5688    0.6408 #>  #> First stage (Z on D): z = 32.93 (z^2 ~ first-stage F = 1084)

title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: treatment outcome may continuous, binary, count, fraction; choose families fitted values respect response’s range — heart IPWRA recommendation: Binary outcomes also accept omodel = "probit", response confined unit interval — proportion rate — uses omodel = "flogit" "fprobit". fractional families share estimation "logit" / "probit" relax response [0, 1], matching Stata lateffects omodel options.

title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: Beyond logit MLE, two estimators tailor propensity score job weights: ivmodel = "cbps" — covariate balancing (Imai Ratkovic 2014): chooses logit coefficients inverse-probability weights exactly balance covariates instrument arms. ivmodel = "ipt" — inverse probability tilting (Graham, Pinto, Egel 2012): fits separate tilted score per arm; resulting weights exactly normalized construction. ivmodel = "probit" (new 0.2.0) — probit maximum likelihood, mirroring kappalate’s zmodel(probit); available weighting estimators ("ipw" kappa methods Section 6).
code: coef(drlate(lwage ~ age + educ, nvstat ~ age + educ, rsncode ~ age + educ,             data = drlate_sim, ivmodel = "ipt"))[1] #> LATE: D on Y  #>    0.4706598  # probit propensity score with a weighting estimator (Section 6) coef(drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1, rsncode ~ age + educ,             data = drlate_sim, method = "kappa10",             ivmodel = "probit"))[1] #> LATE: D on Y  #>    0.4738596

what: 6. Abadie’s kappa: the weighting-estimator menu (new in 0.2.0)
title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: companion paper Słoczyński, Uysal, Wooldridge (2025) studies family pure weighting estimators LATE motivated Abadie’s (2003) kappa theorem. use instrument propensity score — outcome treatment regressions — differ kappa weights use whether weights normalized. drlate implements full menu authors’ Stata command kappalate: kappa methods carry outcome treatment model, first two formulas must intercept-, makes whole menu like--like comparison (every row adjusts covariates instrument propensity score): four sit top — healthy design. paper’s main practical lesson trust disagree: normalized estimators (tau_u, .e. method = "ipw", tau_a,10), unnormalized variants sensitive location scale outcome. can see sensitivity directly. Shift outcome constant, change treatment effect: normalized kappa10 exactly invariant; unnormalized kappa moves. (package prints estimator’s kappalate name mapping always visible output.) Unlike Stata command, drlate’s inference machinery applies kappa menu: cluster-robust standard errors, sampling weights, bootstrap, Fieller confidence sets "kappa" "kappa0" ("kappa10" difference two ratios, Fieller pivot exists, use bootstrap ).
code: cmp_w <- drlate_compare(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1, rsncode ~ age + educ,                         data = drlate_sim,                         methods = c("ipw", "kappa", "kappa0", "kappa10")) cmp_w #> Estimator comparison (LATE) #>  #>  estimator estimate     se           95% CI #>  ipw (nrm)   0.4741 0.0793 [0.3187, 0.6295] #>      kappa   0.4731 0.0800 [0.3164, 0.6298] #>     kappa0   0.4733 0.0803 [0.3159, 0.6306] #>    kappa10   0.4740 0.0793 [0.3186, 0.6294] d_shift <- transform(drlate_sim, lwage = lwage + 100) rbind(   kappa10 = c(original = coef(drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1,                                      rsncode ~ age + educ,                                      data = drlate_sim,                                      method = "kappa10"))[[1]],               shifted = coef(drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1,                                     rsncode ~ age + educ,                                     data = d_shift,                                     method = "kappa10"))[[1]]),   kappa   = c(original = coef(drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1,                                      rsncode ~ age + educ,                                      data = drlate_sim,                                      method = "kappa"))[[1]],               shifted = coef(drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1,                                     rsncode ~ age + educ,                                     data = d_shift,                                     method = "kappa"))[[1]]) ) #>          original   shifted #> kappa10 0.4740434 0.4740434 #> kappa   0.4731112 0.4242734

what: 7. When the instrument is weak: Fieller confidence sets
title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: LATE ratio, ratios imprecise denominators behave badly: usual (delta-method) confidence interval can far--nominal coverage first stage weak. package watches : whenever first-stage F drops 10, printout flags shows Fieller confidence set alongside. Fieller set inverts test H0:num−t⋅denom=0H_0:\ \text{num} - t \cdot \text{denom} = 0 using joint covariance numerator denominator (stacked moment system provides). Unlike Wald interval, keeps promised asymptotic coverage matter weak instrument — honest price may unbounded data barely identify ratio. Watch engage deliberately broken design (instrument shuffled random small subsample, true first stage zero): Fieller set available demand fit: package’s Monte Carlo validation, weak first stage (4% compliers) Fieller set covered true LATE 95.7% time — reporting unbounded set roughly 40% replications, statistically honest answer — Wald interval -covered 99.3%, conveying precision .
code: set.seed(42) d_weak <- drlate_sim[1:300, ] d_weak$zweak <- sample(d_weak$rsncode) fit_weak <- drlate(lwage ~ age, nvstat ~ age, zweak ~ age, data = d_weak) print(fit_weak) #>  #> Local average treatment effect #> Number of obs    : 300 #> Estimator        : IPWRA #> Outcome model    : linear #> Treatment model  : logit #> Instrument model : logit (MLE) #>  #>               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) [95% conf. interval] #> LATE: D on Y 13.662047  118.10943  0.1157   0.9079  -217.8282  245.1523 #> ATE: Z on Y  -0.087409    0.12389 -0.7055   0.4805    -0.3302    0.1554 #> ATE: Z on D  -0.006398    0.05692 -0.1124   0.9105    -0.1180    0.1052 #>  #> First stage (Z on D): z = -0.1124 (z^2 ~ first-stage F = 0.0126)  [weak: Wald inference on the ratio may be unreliable] #> Fieller 95% confidence set for the late: (-Inf, Inf) - the first stage is uninformative confint(fit, method = "fieller")     # strong instrument: ~ Wald interval #> Fieller 95% confidence set for LATE: D on Y: #>   [0.3131, 0.6239]

title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: paper notes inference straightforward “analytically using nonparametric bootstrap.” analytic sandwich default; bootstrap re-runs entire estimation pipeline (including propensity score) resample: Point estimates unchanged; standard errors confidence intervals (now percentile intervals bootstrap distribution) come resamples. cluster supplied, whole clusters resampled. Draws fail — degenerate resamples, overlap violations — dropped counted; non-trivial failure rate sign weak identification, case prefer Fieller set.
code: fit_boot <- drlate(lwage ~ age + educ, nvstat ~ age + educ,                    rsncode ~ age + educ, data = drlate_sim,                    vcov = "bootstrap", boot_reps = 199, boot_seed = 42) summary(fit_boot) #>  #> Local average treatment effect #> Number of obs    : 2,000 #> Estimator        : IPWRA #> Outcome model    : linear #> Treatment model  : logit #> Instrument model : logit (MLE) #> Std. errors      : nonparametric bootstrap (199 of 199 reps) #>  #>              Estimate Std. Error z value   Pr(>|z|) [2.5% boot boot 97.5%] #> LATE: D on Y   0.4705    0.07387   6.369  1.906e-10     0.3240      0.5987 #> ATE: Z on Y    0.2845    0.04748   5.993  2.061e-09     0.1858      0.3746 #> ATE: Z on D    0.6048    0.01852  32.654 7.060e-234     0.5684      0.6417 #>  #> First stage (Z on D): z = 32.93 (z^2 ~ first-stage F = 1084)

what: 10. Do you even need the instrument? The DR Hausman test
title: A primer: doubly robust LATE estimation, from intuition to practice
text: 2022 paper’s Section 5 proposes specification test Stata package implement. logic: one-sided noncompliance (nobody gets treatment without instrument), LATT estimated instrument ATT estimated assuming treatment unconfounded given XX target parameter — unconfoundedness holds. significant difference two doubly robust estimates evidence unconfoundedness. Unlike textbook OLS-vs-IV Hausman test, comparison survives heterogeneous treatment effects. simulated data built confounded treatment (always-takers higher baseline wages), imposing one-sided noncompliance test — — reject: unconfoundedness-based ATT (≈ 0.63) biased upward exactly selection DGP builds , instrument-based LATT (≈ 0.38 one-sided subpopulation) protected. difference almost six standard errors zero. practice: rejection says controls alone suffice — needed instrument; non-rejection says controls may suffice, much precise unconfoundedness-based estimator becomes defensible. standard error difference analytic: estimators’ moment conditions stacked one system correlation accounted — construction paper sketches package carries . (Monte Carlo true null test rejects 4.7% nominal 5% level.)
code: d_os <- drlate_sim d_os$nvstat[d_os$rsncode == 0] <- 0L   # impose one-sided noncompliance  dr_hausman(lwage ~ age + educ, nvstat ~ age + educ, rsncode ~ age + educ,            data = d_os) #>  #>  Doubly robust Hausman test of unconfoundedness #>  (Sloczynski-Uysal-Wooldridge 2022, one-sided noncompliance) #>  #> data:  d_os #> z = -5.7425, p-value = 9.331e-09 #> alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> sample estimates: #>    DR LATT     DR ATT difference  #>  0.3760331  0.6323210 -0.2562878

title: Doubly robust estimation of the LATE and LATT with drlate
text: drlate estimates local average treatment effect (LATE) local average treatment effect treated (LATT) observational data binary instrument. implements complete estimator suite Słoczyński, Uysal, Wooldridge: doubly robust estimators 2022 paper (Stata command drlate, Statistical Software Components S459708) Abadie-kappa weighting estimators 2025 JBES paper (Stata command kappalate, S459257), unified behind one interface one inference architecture. estimation core supports: Doubly robust regression/weighting estimators (method): inverse-probability-weighted regression adjustment ("ipwra", default, doubly robust), inverse probability weighting ("ipw"), augmented inverse probability weighting ("aipw", doubly robust), regression adjustment ("ra"). Abadie-kappa weighting estimators (new 0.2.0): "kappa" (kappalate’s tau_a), "kappa0" (tau_a,0), "kappa10" (tau_a,10); together two IPW variants (= tau_u tau_a,1) complete five-estimator menu 2025 paper. Outcome treatment models: linear, logistic, probit, Poisson, plus fractional-logit fractional-probit outcomes [0, 1], response may continuous, binary, count, proportion (matching Stata lateffects omodel/tmodel options). Instrument propensity score models (ivmodel): logistic regression maximum likelihood (default), covariate balancing ("cbps", Imai Ratkovic 2014), inverse probability tilting ("ipt", Graham, Pinto, Egel 2012), probit maximum likelihood ("probit", weighting estimators; new 0.2.0). Normalized (default) unnormalized weighting IPW AIPW. Sampling weights cluster-robust standard errors. Beyond two Stata commands, package adds common workflow layer, makes available kappa weighting estimators , kappalate offers robust cluster-robust standard errors: Diagnostics: plot() displays propensity-score overlap, covariate balance, implied weights; balance() tables balance_test() overidentification balance test; complier_means() complier profiling; first-stage strength every printout. Weak-instrument-robust inference: Fieller confidence sets via confint(method = "fieller") (ratio-form estimators, including "kappa" "kappa0"). Bootstrap inference: vcov = "bootstrap" (cluster-aware, parallelizable). DR Hausman test unconfoundedness 2022 paper’s Section 5 (dr_hausman()), analytic standard error jointly stacked moment system. Estimator comparison: drlate_compare() dot-whisker plot. estimators validated authors’ Stata commands golden-fixture parity (estimates standard errors), inference extensions Monte Carlo.

text: plot() provides standard design checks — propensity-score overlap, covariate balance /weighting (love plot), implied weight distributions; balance() returns standardized mean differences data frame:   complier_means() profiles compliers differ population (weighting Abadie’s kappa), balance_test() runs Imai–Ratkovic overidentification test whether propensity-score model balances covariates — diagnostics mirror postestimation suite Stata’s lateffects command:
code: fit <- drlate(lwage ~ age + educ, nvstat ~ age + educ,               rsncode ~ age + educ, data = drlate_sim) plot(fit, type = "balance") balance(fit) #>       variable smd_unweighted smd_weighted #> 1          age     0.44613912  0.002276611 #> 2  educcollege     0.07633523 -0.003643849 #> 3 educgraduate     0.18356486  0.008657272 plot(fit, type = "overlap") complier_means(fit) #>       variable population_mean complier_mean   difference #> 1          age         34.5560    34.3303393 -0.225660695 #> 2  educcollege          0.3615     0.3590211 -0.002478943 #> 3 educgraduate          0.1395     0.1431700  0.003670025 balance_test(fit) #> Imai-Ratkovic covariate-balance test (overidentification) #>  #>   Hansen J = 3.0473   df = 4   p-value = 0.5499 #>   Instrument propensity score: logit (n = 2000) #>  #>   H0: the propensity-score model balances the covariates.

title: Doubly robust estimation of the LATE and LATT with drlate
text: Every printout reports first-stage z (z² ≈ F single binary instrument) flags weakness F = 10. package adds two inference tools:
code: # Weak-instrument-robust Fieller confidence set (may be unbounded when # the first stage is weak -- that is the honest answer) confint(fit, method = "fieller") #> Fieller 95% confidence set for LATE: D on Y: #>   [0.3131, 0.6239]  # Nonparametric bootstrap (percentile CIs; clusters resampled whole # when `cluster` is supplied) fit_b <- drlate(lwage ~ age + educ, nvstat ~ age + educ,                 rsncode ~ age + educ, data = drlate_sim,                 vcov = "bootstrap", boot_reps = 199, boot_seed = 1) confint(fit_b) #>                  2.5 %    97.5 % #> LATE: D on Y 0.3178074 0.6159603 #> ATE: Z on Y  0.1965297 0.3794341 #> ATE: Z on D  0.5774018 0.6401410

title: Doubly robust estimation of the LATE and LATT with drlate

Section 5 2022 paper turns equality heterogeneity-robust Hausman test, implemented ; Stata package provide : simulated treatment confounded construction, test rejects.

text: Stata help file’s examples use public extract Survey Income Program Participation (SIPP). equivalent R calls : package’s test suite verifies numerical equivalence estimates standard errors fixtures generated Stata commands dataset (see inst/stata/make-fixtures.inst/stata/make-kappalate-fixtures.).
code: sipp <- haven::read_dta("https://people.brandeis.edu/~tslocz/sipp.dta") sipp <- subset(as.data.frame(sipp),                !is.na(kwage) & !is.na(educ) & rsncode != 999) sipp$lwage <- log(sipp$kwage)  # Stata: drlate (lwage age_5) (nvstat age_5) (rsncode age_5) drlate(lwage ~ age_5, nvstat ~ age_5, rsncode ~ age_5, data = sipp)  # Stata: drlate (lwage age_5) (nvstat age_5) (rsncode age_5, ipt), latt drlate(lwage ~ age_5, nvstat ~ age_5, rsncode ~ age_5, data = sipp,        ivmodel = "ipt", estimand = "latt")  # Stata: kappalate lwage (nvstat = rsncode) age_5, zmodel(logit) which(all) drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1, rsncode ~ age_5, data = sipp,        method = "kappa")     # tau_a; likewise "kappa0", "kappa10",                              # and method = "ipw" for tau_u / tau_a,1  # Stata: kappalate lwage (nvstat = rsncode) age_5, zmodel(probit) drlate(lwage ~ 1, nvstat ~ 1, rsncode ~ age_5, data = sipp,        method = "kappa", ivmodel = "probit")

what: drlate: Doubly Robust LATE and LATT Estimation
title: drlate: Doubly Robust LATE and LATT Estimation
text: drlate provides doubly robust estimation local average treatment effect (LATE) local average treatment effect treated (LATT) observational data binary instrument. implements complete estimator program Słoczyński, Uysal, Wooldridge one package: doubly robust IPWRA/IPW/AIPW/RA estimators Słoczyński, Uysal & Wooldridge (2022) Abadie-kappa weighting estimators Słoczyński, Uysal & Wooldridge (2025, JBES), behind single three-formula interface. Standard errors every estimator come one jointly stacked M-estimation system whose sandwich variance accounts estimation stages, test suite verifies numerical equivalence estimates standard errors fixtures generated authors’ Stata commands (drlate, SSC S459708, kappalate, SSC S459257) across 38 scenarios. Around estimators package provides workflow applied IV analysis needs: design diagnostics, weak-instrument-robust Fieller confidence sets, cluster-aware bootstrap inference, estimator comparison, 2022 paper’s doubly robust Hausman test unconfoundedness.

title: drlate: Doubly Robust LATE and LATT Estimation
text: Abadie-kappa weighting menu Słoczyński, Uysal & Wooldridge (2025, JBES): method = "kappa" (tau_a), "kappa0" (tau_a,0), "kappa10" (tau_a,10), validated Stata kappalate command. drlate’s normalized unnormalized IPW coincide kappalate’s tau_u tau_a,1, completing five-estimator menu. Cluster-robust standard errors, sampling weights, bootstrap, , uniquely, Fieller weak-instrument-robust confidence sets available kappa estimators (Stata command offers robust/cluster standard errors ). Probit instrument propensity scores (ivmodel = "probit", mirroring kappalate’s zmodel(probit)) weighting estimators. , every estimator propensity-score option Stata kappalate command covered fixture-validated. Printed output shows kappalate estimator names throughout. See NEWS full changelog.

text: Tests whether treatment unconfounded given covariates, using comparison proposed Słoczyński, Uysal, Wooldridge (2022, Section 5), building Donald, Hsu, Lieli (2014). one-sided noncompliance (nobody takes treatment without instrument: \(\Pr(D = 1 \mid Z = 0) = 0\)), LATT identified instrument equals ATT identified unconfoundedness treatment — significant difference doubly robust LATT estimate (uses instrument) doubly robust ATT estimate () evidence unconfoundedness. Unlike textbook OLS-vs-IV Hausman test, comparison robust treatment effect heterogeneity.

title: Doubly robust Hausman test of unconfoundedness — dr_hausman
text: DR ATT estimator follows paper's equation (33): treatment propensity score \(\Pr(D = 1 \mid X)\) fitted logit QMLE treatment-equation covariates; outcome model fitted untreated sample weighted odds \(\hat p/(1-\hat p)\); \(\hat\tau_{ATT}\) treated-sample mean outcome minus mean imputed counterfactual. standard error difference comes stacking moment conditions estimators (difference) one M-estimation system, covariance accounted analytically — analytic option suggested paper. Note two halves adjust respective formulas: LATT half's propensity score uses instrument-equation covariates, ATT half's uses treatment-equation covariates (share outcome model). what: drlate: Doubly Robust Estimation of Local Average Treatment Effects — drlate-package
title: drlate: Doubly Robust Estimation of Local Average Treatment Effects — drlate-package
text: Estimates local average treatment effect (LATE) local average treatment effect treated (LATT) using observational data binary instrument, implementing complete estimator suite Sloczynski, Uysal, Wooldridge: doubly robust estimators Sloczynski, Uysal, Wooldridge (2022) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2208.01300  – inverse probability weighted regression adjustment (IPWRA), inverse probability weighting (IPW), augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW), regression adjustment (RA) – Abadie-kappa weighting estimators Sloczynski, Uysal, Wooldridge (2025) doi:10.1080/07350015.2024.2332763 . Supports linear, logistic, probit, Poisson, fractional (fractional-logit fractional-probit) outcome treatment models, instrument propensity scores estimated maximum likelihood, covariate balancing (CBPS), inverse probability tilting (IPT). Standard errors computed jointly estimation stages stacking moment conditions every model single M-estimation system; weak-instrument-robust Fieller confidence sets, cluster-aware bootstrap inference, design diagnostics, doubly robust Hausman-type test unconfoundedness included. Estimates standard errors validated authors' Stata commands 'drlate' (Statistical Software Components S459708) 'kappalate' (S459257).

title: Abadie's kappa weights — kappa_weights
text: Returns per-observation Abadie kappa weight implied fitted drlate() object, $$\kappa = 1 - \frac{D(1 - Z)}{1 - p(X)} - \frac{(1 - D) Z}{p(X)},$$ \(p(X)\) estimated instrument propensity score. kappa weights identify complier subpopulation: function \(g\) data, \(E[g \mid \mathrm{complier}] = E[\kappa g] / E[\kappa]\) (Abadie 2003). weights used complier_means() Stata estat compliers, genkappa() object.

title: drlate 0.3.0
text: New outcome treatment model families, completing parity Stata lateffects omodel/tmodel options: omodel gains "probit" (binary outcome, probit link) "flogit" / "fprobit" fractional outcomes [0, 1] (e.g. proportions rates). tmodel gains "probit". fractional families share estimation binary counterparts relax response unit interval. reuse probit/logit quasi-likelihood scoring already validated instrument propensity score, test suite checks first principles: fractional families coincide binary counterparts 0/1 response, every fit reproduces corresponding weighted glm estimate. Postestimation diagnostics mirroring Stata lateffects suite (StataNow): complier_means() reports population versus complier covariate means, complier averages computed normalized Abadie-kappa weights (Stata’s estat compliers). kappa_weights() returns weights (genkappa object) use complier summaries. balance_test() implements Imai Ratkovic (2014) overidentification test whether instrument propensity score balances covariates (Stata’s latebalance overid); cluster-robust fit . balance(detail = TRUE) adds IPW-weighted arm means unweighted weighted variance ratios alongside standardized mean differences (Stata’s latebalance summarize). plot() gains type = "balance_density" (covariate kernel densities instrument arm, raw versus weighted; Stata’s latebalance density) geom = "density" option type = "overlap" (Stata’s lateoverlap). diagnostics verified standard references: Abadie-kappa identity complier means, Imai Ratkovic (2014) statistic balance test, bootstrap standard errors.

title: drlate 0.2.0
text: New kappa-weighting estimators LATE Słoczyński, Uysal, Wooldridge (2025, JBES 43(1), 164-177): method = "kappa" (tau_a), "kappa0" (tau_a,0), "kappa10" (tau_a,10), validated Stata kappalate command. Cluster-robust SEs, sampling weights, bootstrap, ("kappa"/"kappa0") Fieller confidence sets carry existing machinery. Printed output now shows kappalate names IPW estimators: normalized IPW tau_u, unnormalized IPW tau_a,1. New ivmodel = "probit" (kappalate’s zmodel(probit)) weighting estimators ("ipw" kappa methods), completing coverage kappalate command’s options. drlate_compare() now reports kappa estimator’s normalization normalized column, ?drlate documents kappa denominators kappa-weight means — estimating complier share IPW first-stage contrast, different sample statistic.

title: drlate 0.1.0 (patch)
text: Changes internal econometric audit (Monte Carlo evidence data-raw/mc-review.R data-raw/mc-weak2.R): Correctness (deliberate divergence Stata 1.0.0): unnormalized LATT-AIPW estimator now computes treated share sampling weights. Stata uses unweighted mean , leaves w1 moment condition nonzero pweights invalidates joint variance; uniform weights two coincide exactly (validated configurations unaffected). Fieller sets handle degenerate-quadratic regimes explicitly (half-line denom^2 = q * V_dd, single-point tangency) instead collapsing “whole line”, complement-set print states set unbounded. weak-instrument advisory now triggers first-stage F < 10 (|z| < 3.16) instead |z| < 2, printout reports z^2 ~ F. Cluster bootstrap warns fewer 30 clusters; documentation notes failed bootstrap draws concentrate identification weak (prefer Fieller set regime) seeded results reproducible per fixed number cores. drlate_compare() documents IPW/RA rows use reduced adjustment sets (estimator specification change together) de-duplicates rows normalization auto-switching. dr_hausman() documents LATT ATT halves adjust instrument- treatment-equation covariates respectively.

title: drlate 0.1.0
text: Extensions beyond Stata original: Diagnostics: plot() methods instrument propensity score overlap, covariate balance (love plot), implied-weight distributions; balance() returns standardized mean differences; print()/summary() report first-stage strength flag weak instruments. Bootstrap inference: drlate(vcov = "bootstrap") provides nonparametric bootstrap standard errors percentile confidence intervals (cluster bootstrap cluster supplied), optional parallelism. Weak-instrument-robust inference: confint(method = "fieller") inverts joint test numerator denominator, returning bounded, complement, whole-line confidence sets appropriate. DR Hausman test: dr_hausman() implements doubly robust test unconfoundedness Słoczyński, Uysal & Wooldridge (2022, Section 5) — proposed paper available Stata package — analytic standard error one jointly stacked moment system. Estimator comparison: drlate_compare() runs IPWRA/IPW/AIPW/RA one call comparison table dot-whisker plot. Documentation site: pkgdown website primer vignette featured, deployed via GitHub Actions.

title: drlate 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial R port Stata package drlate v1.0.0 (SSC S459708). LATE LATT estimation via IPWRA, IPW, AIPW, RA. Linear, logit, Poisson outcome/treatment models. Logit (MLE), CBPS, IPT instrument propensity score models. Joint sandwich inference across estimation stages, robust cluster-robust variants sampling weights. Overlap diagnostics (pstolerance, osample). Golden-fixture test harness numerical equivalence Stata: estimates match ~1e-9 standard errors ~1e-6 across 33 validation scenarios.
